problem: Same as PROP-009: the decider's Step E2 pop filter and the curmudgeon's Step 0b skip filter use identical substring match against reviews/. Any target_id with pre-existing review history auto-pops on re-push and auto-skips curmudgeon, silently draining post-integration adversarial review. PROP-009r preserves that diagnosis and the fix direction (strict queue_id + soft reviewed_at > pushed_at fallback) and addresses all 3 critical + 4 major + 3 moderate execution-quality findings from the hostile review.

disagreements_with_review.summary: Tinker agrees with all 10 critical/major/moderate findings and adopts the concrete fixes with two small adjustments. One partial disagreement (C1) is a scope clarification, not a reversal.

c1_partial_disagreement.finding: C1 — the decider find-string is not byte-exact. Patch will fail to apply.
c1_partial_disagreement.tinker_verification: Ran content.includes(PROP-009.find) on both monitor/prompts/decider.md and monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md. Both returned TRUE. The PROP-009 find string, once JSON-decoded from its escaped storage, is already a literal byte-match for the file contents. A naive applier that calls JSON.parse and then uses the resulting string as a find target would apply the patch correctly.
c1_partial_disagreement.tinker_position: The hostile review is correct that the escape convention should be explicit — an applier tool that doesn't JSON-decode the string would fail. But PROP-009 as filed was not broken in the way C1 described. Tinker adopts the array-of-raw-lines format anyway, per the directive's format_constraints, because (a) it is unambiguous regardless of applier implementation, (b) it makes dry_run_check preflights mechanical, and (c) it matches the directive's explicit requirement. So C1 is marked 'fixed_as_specified' in the resolution table — the format change is adopted — but tinker notes for the operator's benefit that the underlying patch would have worked.
c1_partial_disagreement.impact: None on the PROP. Adopting the format is pure defense-in-depth.

disagreements_with_review.m1_operator_bypass_accepted_as_specified: The directive marks the 3-field bypass (pushed_by+require_matching_review_file:false+operator_bypass_reason) as NEGOTIABLE, inviting tinker to argue for silent bypasses if operationally better. Tinker's position: REQUIRE the reason field. Auditability is cheap (one extra string at push time) and the bypass is already advertised as emergency-only; a reason field is consistent with the rest of the project's insistence that structural holes get documented. Silent bypasses would be an observability regression that would show up in tinker's own audits as 'why did qid N pop without a review'.
disagreements_with_review.mod2_seven_day_escalation_removed_from_s: The directive offers a choice: remove from summary (default) or implement the full multi-writer attention-inbox write as a counter-proposal. Tinker takes the default (remove) for three reasons: (a) multi-writer attention-inbox with pull-rebase + JSON merge + retry is its own ~80-line node block that deserves its own PROP and its own hostile review; (b) the log-only fallback is not dead code if enforcement is ever flipped — 7d stale items WILL accumulate under enforcement and a log line is visible to tinker's Mode 2 error-pattern scan; (c) conflating two architectural changes in one commit makes rollback harder, which is the exact coordination-fragility failure mode Mod3 addresses. A future PROP-011 can add the real escalation once the shadow→enforce migration completes cleanly.
disagreements_with_review.mod3_apply_order_adopted: Curmudgeon first, decider second, flag third. Tinker agrees this is strictly safer than single-commit because it provides three natural rollback points. Tinker adds one note: between step 1 (curmudgeon patch lands) and step 3 (decider patch lands), existing decider runs still use the old substring match, so no unmatched-pops accumulate during the gap.
disagreements_with_review.m2_consolidation_note: Tinker adopts the full 6-location queue_id threading. Considered consolidating to 3 locations (Step 0b line 2, Step 7 schema, Step 7b validator) but concluded the three target_type-specific edits (Step 4 proposal, Step 4b prediction-batch, and the inline line 7 reminder) each carry independent load-bearing meaning and should not be dropped. 6 edits total.

C-1_escape_correctness_from_hostile_revi.verdict: Hostile review #1 C-1 was factually wrong. PROP-009 find string escape was correct.
C-1_escape_correctness_from_hostile_revi.raw_json_bytes_at_backslash_d_region: Offset 11297-11301 of PROP-009.json: char codes 92, 92, 100, 43 — i.e. the two-char ASCII sequence "\\" followed by "d+". In the JSON source file this appears as "\\d+".
C-1_escape_correctness_from_hostile_revi.json_parse_decoded_string: After JSON.parse, those bytes become the four-char string: "\d+" (one backslash, lowercase d, plus). Verified via node: `[...p.proposed_fix.files_to_modify[0].find.substring(641,645)].map(c=>c.charCodeAt(0))` returned `[92, 100, 43, 91]` — one backslash, d, +, [.
C-1_escape_correctness_from_hostile_revi.decider_md_bytes_at_same_region: At the `secMatch=tid.match(/^part(` anchor, the next 4 bytes of decider.md are `\d+[` (char codes 92, 100, 43, 91). One literal backslash.
C-1_escape_correctness_from_hostile_revi.match_result: String equality: JSON.parse(PROP-009.find).substring(641,645) === decider.md.substring(similar_region, +4). `content.includes(find)` returns TRUE. The escape convention PROP-009 used was correct.
C-1_escape_correctness_from_hostile_revi.what_went_wrong_in_hostile_1: Hostile reviewer #1 saw "\\d+" in the JSON source (4-char appearance) and assumed that was the decoded string, not the JSON-encoded bytes. Reading the raw JSON source at face value (without mentally applying JSON.parse) gives the wrong byte count.
C-1_escape_correctness_from_hostile_revi.why_we_adopted_dry_run_check_anyway: Defense in depth. Even though C-1 was factually wrong, having preflights per block catches a DIFFERENT class of bug — anchor drift between PROP draft time and apply time. This run proved the value when the 11:50Z verification pass caught 3 of 9 anchors mismatched against main HEAD. Hostile #2 block-7 shell-quote bug is yet another bug class the preflights caught (or would have caught if tinker #1 had actually executed them end-to-end).

C-2_shadow_mode_claim_path_worked_exampl.verdict: Hostile review #2 C-2 conflates the real-match path with the legacyRev path. The current code already writes popped_by_queue_id for real matches regardless of enforce/shadow mode. Only legacyRev pops skip the claim write, and that is by design.
C-2_shadow_mode_claim_path_worked_exampl.current_code_behavior: In block 1 replace_lines of PROP-009r, the `if(shouldPop)` branch calls `claimsToWrite.push({file,qid})` AND does the in-memory `r.popped_by_queue_id=item.queue_id` patch WHENEVER realMatch is non-null — no enforce/shadow gate. The post-filter flush loop writes popped_by_queue_id to the review file dual-write (WORKSPACE + CLONE). The only `if(!enforce)` gate in the filter body is inside the `legacyRev` branch, which handles reviews that have NO queue_id AND fail the reviewed_at>pushed_at test — that is legacy substring-only match, by definition not a real match.
worked_example_shadow_soft_match_reuse: Setup: queue has qid 100 (target_id WIN-003, pushed_at 10:00Z) and qid 101 (target_id WIN-003, pushed_at 10:30Z). Review file WIN-003.c1.json exists with queue_id=undefined and reviewed_at=11:00Z.
- available = all reviews with popped_by_queue_id in {null, 100}. Includes WIN-003.c1.json.
- strictRev = available.find(r => r.queue_id === 100). None — queue_id undefined.
- softRev = available.find(r => searchTerms include "WIN-003" && r.reviewed_at > 10:00Z). MATCH: WIN-003.c1.json (11:00 > 10:00).
- realMatch = softRev. shouldPop = true. claimsToWrite.push({file:"WIN-003.c1.json", qid:100}). In-memory r.popped_by_queue_id = 100.
End of run 1: flush writes popped_by_queue_id=100 and popped_by_queue_id_at=<now> onto WIN-003.c1.json in both WORKSPACE and CLONE.
- available = reviews with popped_by_queue_id in {null, 101}. WIN-003.c1.json has popped_by_queue_id=100, so EXCLUDED.
- strictRev, softRev both null for WIN-003.c1.json.
- Falls through to legacyRev — also null (excluded from available).
- qid 101 stays in queue (no match, no pop). CORRECT BEHAVIOR.
Result: single review does NOT claim both pushes. C3's invariant holds in shadow mode for real matches. C-2 as stated in hostile #2 is factually wrong.
C-2_shadow_mode_claim_path_worked_exampl.what_we_did_anyway: Added an explicit doc comment in the Step E2 bash preamble documenting this invariant, so a future editor cannot accidentally wrap the `if(shouldPop)` claim path in `if(enforce)`. Cheap insurance.
C-2_shadow_mode_claim_path_worked_exampl.caveat: If hostile reviewer #2 was actually concerned about the legacyRev-then-shadow-pop path (where shadow mode pops with pop_reason:"shadow_legacy_substring" and claimed_review_file:null), that IS by design: legacyRev means no queue_id AND no reviewed_at>pushed_at, so binding popped_by_queue_id to it would be a lie (the review was written BEFORE the push, so it cannot service that push). PROP-009r2 leaves legacyRev-shadow-pop claim-less.

fresh_retroactive_audit.method: Read monitor/curmudgeon/priority-queue.json history (50 most recent entries, span 2026-04-14T12:27Z through 2026-04-18T09:41Z — the file's slice cap). For each history entry classify as strict_queue_id (review file exists with matching queue_id), soft (substring match + reviewed_at within 2h at-or-before popped_at), or bug (no match). Soft window chosen per directive guidance to match the per-day breakdown from the concrete-fixes agent.
fresh_retroactive_audit.tinker_count_vs_concrete_fixes_agent: IDENTICAL — 23/50 unmatched, matching per-day breakdown (04-14: 6, 04-15: 4, 04-16: 1, 04-17: 3, 04-18: 9). No classification errors detected. Uses the same 2h-at-or-before-popped_at soft criterion.
fresh_retroactive_audit.recommended_enforce_flip_threshold: ≤3 would-have-blocked items per day, averaged over 3 consecutive days, with no single day above 6. Rationale: historical bug rate is 5.75/day (23/4 days); 5.75 is inside the noise of the worst single-day numbers (04-18: 9, 04-14: 6). The gate must be strictly below the historical rate to prove strict_queue_id is now populating correctly. ≤3/day with a 3-day moving window is roughly half the historical rate — high enough not to be unreachable, low enough to demonstrate the new pathway is working.
fresh_retroactive_audit.rejection_of_prop_009_original_threshold: PROP-009's ≤5/day threshold was uncalibrated. At ≤5/day, 04-14 (6), 04-15 (4), and 04-17 (3) would all be classified 'inside target', which means flipping enforcement would be allowed under purely historical noise — no proof that the new queue_id pathway is working. Revised threshold rejects that.

Md-2_mixed_version_step_7b_validator.evidence: Added explicit migration_plan note: the Step 7b validator change is landed in the SAME commit as the rest of curmudgeon.md (step 1), not deferred. But pre-patch-in-flight curmudgeon runs finishing during the gap have no `queue_id` in their review JSON — so the validator will throw. Mitigation (chosen): extend the validator to WARN (console.error + continue) rather than throw for the first 24h after step-1 commit, then upgrade to throw. Implementation: the validator inspects file mtime of the ENFORCE flag or a dedicated `queue_id_validator_strict_at` field in prop-009-enforce.flag if present; absence = warn. Simpler alternative: commit the validator behind a `process.env.CURMUDGEON_STRICT_QUEUE_ID=1` gate and flip it at the same time as the enforce flag. PROP-009r2 adopts the warn-then-throw approach, with a dedicated timestamp-gated guard inside the validator inline.

orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.observation: SEC-4.1.c3.json file mtime (FUSE workspace): 2026-04-18T00:16:21Z. SEC-4.1.c4.json file mtime (FUSE workspace): 2026-04-18T06:12:46Z. c3 was written ~5h56m BEFORE c4 — consistent with its lower cycle number. Cycle numbering is NOT backwards by filesystem evidence. The real anomaly is the SELF-CONTRADICTORY internal timestamp on c3: `reviewed_at=2026-04-18T07:42:00Z` is 7h26m AFTER its own mtime=00:16:21Z. c4 also has a smaller inverted gap: `reviewed_at=2026-04-18T05:12:46.475Z` is ~1h BEFORE its own mtime=06:12:46Z.
orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.revised_likely_cause: Two possibilities: (a) curmudgeon writes the review file with a placeholder reviewed_at (e.g., the queue item's pushed_at from an earlier queue snapshot), then later updates it without touching the file mtime. (b) clock skew between the agent process clock and the FUSE filesystem clock. (a) is more likely because c3's queue_id=158 corresponds to a queue push at 2026-04-17T23:26Z — and c3's internal reviewed_at=07:42Z looks like it could be a plausible "when curmudgeon finished thinking" timestamp that does not match the "when curmudgeon wrote the file" clock. The investigation should check curmudgeon.md Step 7a/b for where reviewed_at is generated.
orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.previous_tinker_error_in_PROP_009r: Tinker #1 (PROP-009r author) looked at the internal reviewed_at fields alone and concluded "c4 was written 2.5h BEFORE c3". That read is wrong — internal reviewed_at is not a reliable proxy for write time, because the internal field can be set from a variety of sources (pushed_at, "when agent started", manual edits) and is not filesystem-bound. File mtime is authoritative for write time. Hostile review #2 C-3 caught this.
orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.scope_classification: ORTHOGONAL to this PROP. Fixing the pop/skip bug does not repair self-contradictory reviewed_at values, and conversely. Filed as an ISS in open-issues.json (see files_to_create below); needs its own PROP if pattern recurs in more than 3 reviews over the next 7 days.
orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.severity_if_unfixed: moderate — misleads downstream consumers (decider triage, tinker audit, integrity checks) about review chronology, and distorts the reviewed_at>pushed_at soft-fallback comparison in this PROP's Step E2 pop filter. The soft-fallback can match a review whose stated reviewed_at is AFTER pushed_at even though the file was written BEFORE the push — a rare case but a real one.
orthogonal_finding_cycle_numbering_backw.verification_method: stat -c '%y %n' on the FUSE workspace path (mount-preserved mtimes), cross-referenced against `git log --follow --format=%aI` on the clone path (which shows only the workspace-sync commit time, NOT the original write time).

proposed_fix.summary: Replace the decider's Step E2 pop filter and the curmudgeon's Step 0b Procedure line 2 skip filter with a precondition that requires one of: (a) a review file whose queue_id field exactly matches the queue item's queue_id (strict); or (b) a review file whose target_id substring-matches AND whose reviewed_at post-dates the queue item's pushed_at (soft fallback for legacy reviews without queue_id tags). If neither matches, leave the item in place (enforced mode) or pop-with-shadow-log (shadow mode). Decider writes popped_by_queue_id onto the matched review file at pop-time (dual-write WORKSPACE+CLONE, additive only) to prevent one review from claiming multiple pushes. Decider reads reviews/ from BOTH workspace (FUSE) AND clone, union-merged, to close the FUSE staleness race. Enforcement toggled by presence of monitor/decisions/prop-009-enforce.flag. Narrow operator bypass requires pushed_by contains 'operator' AND require_matching_review_file:false AND operator_bypass_reason non-empty string. Stale-item handling is log-only in this PROP (attention-inbox escalation deferred to future PROP-011). Curmudgeon threads queue_id and queue_pushed_at through 6 locations in its prompt so every queue-sourced review carries the identifier the decider uses to match.

**Step E2 precondition (PROP-009, enforced default).** The pop filter requires a matching review file. "Matching" is strict queue_id OR (soft) substring + `reviewed_at > pushed_at`. This precondition is ALWAYS ON — including for operator-pushed items. Substring alone never pops.
**Narrow operator bypass.** An operator push may bypass only by setting ALL THREE of the following on the queue item JSON at push time:
2. `require_matching_review_file: false` (explicit opt-out — must be literal boolean false, not missing).
3. `operator_bypass_reason: "<non-empty string explaining why>"` (auditable justification; appears in shadow log and in history `pop_reason`).

All three missing? Precondition applies normally. Any one missing? Precondition applies normally — the bypass is all-or-nothing and requires the explicit reason field. Bypasses are logged to `monitor/integrity/prop-009-shadow.jsonl` with `pop_reason: "operator_bypass"` so tinker can audit frequency.

The operator MUST NOT use bypass to push items faster than curmudgeon can review. Bypass is for emergency cleanup (e.g., queue entry whose target was deleted and cannot be reviewed); it is not a throughput tool. More than 3 `operator_bypass` pops in 24h is flagged by tinker's daily Mode 2 run.

**Enforcement toggle.** Presence of `monitor/decisions/prop-009-enforce.flag` (clone-side) = enforced. Absence = shadow. Flip with `touch monitor/decisions/prop-009-enforce.flag && git add monitor/decisions/prop-009-enforce.flag && git commit -m 'PROP-009: enforce'`. Roll back with `git rm monitor/decisions/prop-009-enforce.flag && git commit -m 'PROP-009: back to shadow'`.

Check if any filename contains the item's `target_id`. For example, queue item `target_id: "WIN-003"` is already reviewed if `WIN-003.c2.json` (or any file matching `WIN-003*`) exists. Queue item `target_id: "EXP-050"` is already reviewed if `EXP-050-proposal.c2.json` exists. For `EXP-054-batch`, check for `EXP-054-batch*.json`. replace_lines: 2. Find the **first un-reviewed** item in `queue` — strict FIFO, no reordering. **PROP-009 precondition:** an item is 'already reviewed' ONLY if one of the following holds (read from `${WORKSPACE}/monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/`, not the clone):
- **Strict:** a review file exists whose top-level `queue_id` field equals this item's `queue_id`; OR

- **Soft fallback (for legacy reviews without queue_id tags):** a review file exists whose filename substring-matches the `target_id` AND whose `reviewed_at` timestamp post-dates this item's `pushed_at` timestamp.

Substring-match alone is NOT sufficient — a pre-existing old review for the same `target_id` does not count. For example, queue item `target_id: "WIN-058"` with `queue_id: 166` and `pushed_at: "2026-04-18T07:26Z"` is NOT covered by `WIN-058.c2.json` from 2026-04-08, even though the filename substring-matches. You MUST review this item.

When you DO write a review for a queue-sourced item, **always include the `queue_id` field** (integer) and the `queue_pushed_at` field (ISO timestamp) inside the review JSON, copied verbatim from the queue item — this is how the decider's Step E2 pop filter finds your work and prevents post-integration adversarial review from being silently skipped. Do not rely on the filename alone to carry queue identity. Cycle-number determination (`.c2`, `.c3`, `.c4`) should still follow the existing rule (highest existing cycle + 1), but the `queue_id` field disambiguates which push this review covers.
For section items like `part3-3.1b`, the search-terms expansion (`SEC-3.1b*`) still applies to the substring portion of the soft fallback.
**If ALL queue items either (a) strict-match or (b) soft-match under the above rule, skip to Step 0c** — the queue is fully reviewed and the decider will clean it up on its next run.

replace_lines: ### 7a. Pre-write assertion — queue_id presence (PROP-009, mandatory for queue-sourced reviews)

Before you finalize the review file, verify: "Is this review servicing a priority-queue item (Step 0b)? If yes, is `queue_id` set on the review JSON to the integer value from the queue entry I am reviewing? Is `queue_pushed_at` set to the queue entry's `pushed_at`?" If either answer is "no, but it should be", stop and re-read the queue entry. This is the load-bearing identifier — missing or wrong `queue_id` silently breaks the decider's pop filter.

**After writing the review file, immediately parse it to confirm validity.** Invalid JSON breaks the decider's digest pipeline silently — review findings become inaccessible. PROP-009 extends the validator to assert `queue_id` presence on queue-sourced reviews.

In the command below, substitute `<filename>.json` with the EXACT basename of the file you just wrote (e.g., `SEC-4.1.c5.json` or `EXP-183-proposal.json`). The `<filename>` is a literal placeholder the agent MUST replace; do not run the command verbatim.

node -e "const d=JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/<filename>.json','utf8'));const known=new Set(['change-detected','holistic','spot-check','priority-queue-win-rewrite','priority-queue-section-rewrite','priority-queue-expansion-proposal','priority-queue-expansion-prose','priority-queue-prediction-batch']);const t=d.trigger;if(t&&!known.has(t))console.error('WARN: unknown trigger '+t+' — queue_id strictness skipped');else if(t&&t.startsWith('priority-queue-')&&!Number.isInteger(d.queue_id)){const strictAfter=Date.parse(d.reviewed_at||'1970')>Date.parse(process.env.QUEUE_ID_STRICT_AFTER||'2026-04-20T00:00:00Z');if(strictAfter)throw new Error('queue-sourced review missing queue_id');else console.error('WARN: queue-sourced review missing queue_id (strict after '+(process.env.QUEUE_ID_STRICT_AFTER||'2026-04-20T00:00:00Z')+')');}console.log('valid')"
- `known` enumerates the accepted `trigger` values. Missing `trigger` is tolerated (returns 'valid' without strictness, preserves pre-PROP-009 review compatibility).
- Unknown `trigger` strings (future additions not yet in `known`) emit a WARN and skip the queue_id strictness — safer than throwing during agent rollout.

- Queue-sourced triggers (`priority-queue-*`) require `queue_id` as an integer. During the 24h migration window after the curmudgeon.md commit lands, the validator WARN-logs instead of throwing (controlled by the `QUEUE_ID_STRICT_AFTER` env var, default 2026-04-20T00:00:00Z). The decider's pre-push validator should also honor this env for pre-patch-in-flight reviews.

files_to_modify.explanation: C-1: registers monitor/decisions/prop-009-enforce.flag in workspace-sync.md OWNED_BY_GIT so workspace-sync short-circuits any attempt to push the workspace copy of the flag back to git. Keeps the `git rm && commit` rollback authoritative and prevents a stale FUSE copy from silently reverting a rollback. Paired with build.js block 10; both must land together.

files_to_create.purpose: Drop-in ISS JSON the decider should append to monitor/decisions/open-issues.json on its next run. Captures the self-contradictory reviewed_at anomaly as a trackable issue (corrected from PROP-009r which mis-diagnosed it as a cycle-numbering race).
files_to_create.content:
{
  "id": "ISS-AUTO-FROM-PROP-009r2",
  "created_at": "2026-04-18T12:55:00Z",
  "source": "PROP-009r2 orthogonal finding",
  "severity": "moderate",
  "target_type": "pipeline_bug",
  "target_id": "curmudgeon-self-contradictory-reviewed-at",
  "title": "Curmudgeon review JSON reviewed_at can contradict the file mtime by hours",
  "description": "SEC-4.1.c3.json file mtime 2026-04-18T00:16:21Z but its internal reviewed_at is 2026-04-18T07:42:00Z — 7h26m AFTER the file was written. SEC-4.1.c4.json file mtime 2026-04-18T06:12:46Z but its internal reviewed_at is 2026-04-18T05:12:46.475Z — ~1h BEFORE the file was written. This contradiction is NOT a cycle-numbering race (cycle numbers track file mtimes correctly: c3 mtime < c4 mtime). The anomaly is inside each review file: reviewed_at does not match write time. Impact: PROP-009's soft-fallback pop filter compares reviewed_at > pushed_at; a self-contradictory reviewed_at can cause a review written BEFORE a push to falsely appear to service that push (reviewed_at > pushed_at satisfied on stale timestamp, even though the file was written first).",
  "evidence_files": [
    "monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/SEC-4.1.c3.json",
    "monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/SEC-4.1.c4.json"
  ],
  "recommended_fix": "Investigate where curmudgeon.md Step 7 generates reviewed_at. Likely candidates: (a) curmudgeon copies pushed_at from the queue snapshot it loaded at run start, not fs.statSync(reviewPath).mtime; (b) an agent process clock drifts from the FUSE filesystem clock; (c) the review file is written in two passes with an earlier draft using an initial timestamp. Fix: set reviewed_at to `new Date().toISOString()` at the exact moment of fs.writeFileSync, not earlier. Needs its own PROP.",
  "assignee": "tinker",
  "blocks_prop_009r2": false,
  "status": "open"

files_to_create.instruction: Decider should append the top-level object to the `issues` array of monitor/decisions/open-issues.json on its next intake pass. After appending, decider assigns a real ISS-NNN id and updates this PROP's related.orthogonal_pending_followup field to cross-reference. Per PROP-009r2 Mn-2 fix: after appending, the decider MUST also `git rm monitor/decisions/open-issues-PROP-009r2-append.json` in the same commit so the ledger does not grow unbounded. This directory is append_only_glob, but the seeded one-shot intake file is a decider-created artifact and decider is authorized to remove its own intake files.

migration_plan.alternative_considered_immediate_enforce: REJECTED — immediate enforcement risks creating a queue backlog if curmudgeon's first run after patch doesn't include queue_id. Shadow period lets us confirm the curmudgeon side is also working before pops become strict.
migration_plan.alternative_considered_single_commit: REJECTED — hostile review's 'coordination fragility' finding plus Mod3's rollback-points argument both favor the multi-commit sequence. Single-commit is only a marginal simplicity gain and costs two natural rollback points. PROP-009r2 keeps the 3-commit sequence but now commit 1 carries the build.js and workspace-sync.md changes alongside curmudgeon.md for atomic ownership registration.
audit_criteria_before_flipping_enforce: Denominator is NEW reviews: those whose reviewed_at > commit timestamp of step-1 commit. Legacy reviews (older than step-1) are excluded from numerator AND denominator. Target: ≥80% of NEW reviews with priority-queue-* triggers carry queue_id as an integer.

Over a rolling 3-day window of pq.history entries, strict_queue_id pops must constitute the MAJORITY (>50%) of all pop_reason values. The 'at least one strict entry exists' bar is insufficient (trivially satisfiable).

Shadow log shows ≤3 distinct (queue_id, pushed_at) tuples blocked per day over 3 consecutive days, with no single day above 6. (Distinct tuples, not entry count — Mj-2 dedupe makes this a count of stuck items, not decider runs.)
Every would-have-blocked tuple corresponds to a legitimately-unreviewed push (not a pop that should have happened).
No filesystem_read_counts.clone lagging fuse by >5 entries for >1 hour (confirms workspace-sync is keeping up).

tinker_mode_2_bypass_audit.purpose: Md-3 implementation — bind the "more than 3 bypasses in 24h" threshold to a concrete query instead of leaving it aspirational.
tinker_mode_2_bypass_audit.procedure: During Tinker Mode 2 daily run, count operator_bypass entries in monitor/integrity/prop-009-shadow.jsonl over the last 24h. Pseudocode: `const log = require("fs").readFileSync("monitor/integrity/prop-009-shadow.jsonl","utf8").split("\n").filter(l=>l.trim()).map(l=>{try{return JSON.parse(l);}catch{return null;}}).filter(Boolean); const cutoff = Date.now() - 86400000; const bypasses = log.filter(e => e.pop_reason === "operator_bypass" && Date.parse(e.logged_at) > cutoff); if (bypasses.length > 3) flag as "moderate — operator bypass abuse suspect";`
tinker_mode_2_bypass_audit.output: Finding in Tinker Mode 2 report if threshold crossed. No new code in decider needed.

migration_plan.md_2_mixed_version_step_7b_validator_not: The Step 7b validator change (block 7) lands in step 1 alongside the rest of curmudgeon.md. During the 24h migration window (until QUEUE_ID_STRICT_AFTER env var date, default 2026-04-20T00:00:00Z), the validator WARN-logs instead of throwing when a queue-sourced review lacks queue_id. This lets pre-patch-in-flight curmudgeon runs finish without blocking the commit. After the cutoff, the validator throws. The cutoff is NOT tied to the step-3 decider commit — it is a fixed wall-clock time to avoid migration coupling.

tradeoffs: GAIN: post-integration adversarial reviews are no longer silently skipped. Soft-fallback-reuse bug (C3) cannot occur because popped_by_queue_id consumption tracking is strict. Operator bypass is explicit and auditable, not silent. FUSE staleness race (M4) is closed via union-read. Retroactive audit gives an evidence-based threshold (M3) instead of a guess. Cycle-number race (Mod1) is tracked rather than lost. LOSE: ~60 extra lines of Opus context in decider.md Step E2 (unionReviewFiles + consumption tracking + dual-write adds length), mitigated by the fact that Step E2 runs once per decider invocation. RISK: the dual-write of popped_by_queue_id to WORKSPACE and CLONE means two writes per pop; on a slow FUSE this adds ~20-50ms. RISK: flag-file-based enforcement is racy if the operator touches the flag mid-decider-run; documented but acceptable (operator can retry). RISK: the 3-commit apply order means there's a window (between step 1 and step 3) where curmudgeon writes queue_id but decider still uses substring match — fine (old path continues to work) but a third party reading commits out of order may be confused.

estimated_impact.token_cost: decider.md Step E2 grows from ~35 lines to ~120 lines. Runs once per decider invocation (~5-10 times/day). Net: <1000 extra tokens per day on Opus. Curmudgeon.md grows by ~15 lines across 7 edits; runs every curmudgeon invocation (~5-24 times/day). Net: <300 extra tokens per day.
estimated_impact.quality_gain: critical — closes the post-integration adversarial review gap. Directly prevents the SEC-4.1/WIN-058 class regression documented in the directive. Closes C3 multi-push-single-review double-claim risk (identified by hostile review; not in original PROP-009).
estimated_impact.risk_reduction: high — the original bug was silent (no error, no log, no alert). Shadow log + filesystem_read_counts on every entry creates observability for FUSE/clone sync gaps as a side benefit.

why_human_needed: Operator must (a) approve the revised precondition wording (strict queue_id + soft reviewed_at > pushed_at + narrow 3-field operator bypass), (b) approve the 3-commit apply order, (c) acknowledge the Mod1 ISS entry will be filed by decider (no separate approval needed — decider is authoritative for open-issues.json writes), (d) approve the Mod2 decision to defer full attention-inbox escalation to a future PROP-011, (e) acknowledge the Mod3 apply-order vs single-commit choice (Mod3 recommended).
